behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real OS filesystem watcher semantics for recursive directory events. A mock watcher can hide platform-specific recursion gaps. Not Agentic/Workflow because no AI/ML decision is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and verify via output-directory readback that exactly one transformed result is produced for that nested file.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: The risk is module handoff/state interaction (watch event intake + dedup cache + pipeline dispatch), which is beyond single-function logic but does not require real OS/network/subprocess if requests are injected in-process. Rejected System because the behavior text does not require external contract/error-shape checks or real-OS-only effects.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the real dedup and dispatch modules in-process with a controllable clock, submit two requests for the same path inside the configured window, and assert only one dispatch to transformation/output occurs; then advance time beyond the window and assert a new dispatch is allowed.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is explicitly OS event behavior; in-process fakes can miss duplicate-event patterns from real watchers. This meets REAL-INFRA and requires proving external side effect count via round-trip observation. Not Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: With a real filesystem watcher active, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write + rename), capture processing/output records, and assert exactly one completed transformation/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Directory deletion/recreation handling depends on real OS watcher lifecycle behavior and re-subscription after inode/path changes. Mocks often miss this. No AI/ML content dependency, so not Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real watched directory, remove that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify the daemon processes it without process restart by checking resulting output.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp behavior (resolution, ordering ties) plus watcher arrival races. Integration with mocked metadata can miss real ordering edge cases. Not Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files near-simultaneously in the real watched directory with controlled timestamp differences, run the daemon, and verify observed processing/output order matches creation-timestamp order (including defined tie-break behavior if timestamps are equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real I/O timing/locking/partial-write condition at the OS/filesystem boundary. In-process mocks usually cannot reproduce the exact race and visibility semantics. No LLM dependency, so not Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file incrementally (or hold an open writer) in the watched directory to trigger early arrival events, assert the daemon does not process partial content, then close/finish the write and verify processing occurs once with complete content.
